question: Does the NuScale approach to eliminating conventional safety systems (Table 1.3-2) with passive alternatives provide an adequate safety level, considering Ch.05 DHRS performance (Figure 5.4-7 and performance curves)?

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified instrumentation, control, electrical, and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside written text. It lists the relevant diagrams, legends, and tables used for both electrical/I&C and piping/instrumentation systems, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details the classification and exercising of actuation valves, specifies when and how testing is performed (including during cold shutdown conditions), and describes a one-time in-situ test of the DHRS function during initial reactor startup.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The section describes the safety features of the NuScale reactor, highlighting the benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), such as reducing corrosion, aiding leak detection, and minimizing heat transfer and combustible gases. It emphasizes the reactor’s passive safety systems, simplified design, and small fuel inventory, which together lower the risk of core damage and eliminate the need for external power during accidents, resulting in improved safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures that list and explain the diagrams and symbols used, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are thoroughly evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch01_0558.title: DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0: Radiation Protection Design FeaturesII.9 RG 8.15
nuscale_ch01_0558.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory guides related to radiation protection design features, as outlined in DSRS 12.3-12.4, Rev 0. It details whether each guide is applicable, and if so, whether NuScale fully or partially conforms, with references to supporting sections or tables for further information. Several guides are noted as not applicable, while others are partially or fully met.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and nominal parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It highlights key differences in areas such as electrical output, core size, safety systems, and reactor components, using summary tables for direct comparison. The data provided is nominal and intended for illustrative purposes only.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems, simplified integral design, and small fuel inventory eliminate large-break LOCA scenarios and reduce reliance on external power, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup power equipment. It also highlights human factors considerations, emphasizing fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0066.title: Material Referenced
nuscale_ch01_0066.summary: This section lists the topical and technical reports incorporated by reference in the NuScale Power Plant Design Certification Application, as detailed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover various aspects such as quality assurance, risk significance, safety classification, accident analysis methodologies, and nuclear analysis codes, and are referenced throughout relevant sections of the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR).

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design provides passive removal of decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which remain closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water back to the core, with all cooling functions relying on passive heat conduction to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale reactor’s core and cooling system design. It explains the natural circulation process of reactor coolant for heat transfer, the generation of superheated steam to drive a turbine and produce electricity, and the role of the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) in supporting auxiliary systems and heat removal. Specific equipment and cooling tower details are site-dependent and further described elsewhere in the report.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the relevant figures and tables that define safe operating conditions to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor vessel.

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a containment vessel. The RPV is a critical pressure-retaining component that forms part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary and is constructed from materials specified by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design lifetime.

nuscale_ch01_0853.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.2.B Review Criteria for Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation)
nuscale_ch01_0853.summary: This section confirms that NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report aligns with the review criteria in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0 for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, superseding previous guidance (DI&C ISG-05). It notes full conformance for all phases of D3 analysis, while partial conformance is indicated for probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) aspects, which are the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0852.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.1.B Review Criteria for Phase 1 (Analysis)
nuscale_ch01_0852.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale’s approach aligns with the updated review criteria for all phases of D3 analysis, superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0738.title: SRP 15.4.3, Rev 3: Control Rod Misoperation (System Malfunction or Operator Error)II.1 Thermal Margin Limits
nuscale_ch01_0738.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria regarding control rod misoperation and related thermal margin limits. NuScale meets the requirements by using appropriate heat generation rate limits and the 95/95 MCHFR approach to ensure fuel and cladding integrity, noting that some criteria are not applicable due to differences in reactor design. The terminology and analysis methods are adapted to fit NuScale's specific technology, and certain guidance for other reactor types does not apply.

nuscale_ch01_0732.title: SRP 15.4.1, Rev 3: Uncontrolled Control Rod Assembly Withdrawal From a Subcritical or Low Power Startup ConditionII.1.A Thermal Margin Limits
nuscale_ch01_0732.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for various reactor accident scenarios, highlighting that its passive natural circulation design eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps, making certain SRP sections not applicable. For uncontrolled control rod withdrawal events, NuScale uses critical heat flux (CHF) and heat generation rate limits to ensure thermal margin and fuel integrity, aligning with regulatory expectations.

nuscale_ch01_0856.title: SRP 19.0, Rev 3: Probabilistic Risk Assessment and Severe Accident Evaluation for New ReactorsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0856.summary: The section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with NRC regulatory criteria related to probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) and severe accident evaluation for new reactors. It notes full conformance with criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, while indicating only partial conformance with SRP 19.0 requirements, as site-specific hazard evaluation and PRA updates are the responsibility of the COL (Combined License) applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0857.title: SRP 19.1, Rev 3: Determining The Technical Adequacy of Probabilistic Risk Assessment For Risk-Informed License Amendment Requests After Initial Fuel LoadAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0857.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with NRC regulatory criteria related to human factors engineering and probabilistic risk assessment (PRA). It notes that NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report aligns with most review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while partial conformance is indicated for PRA and severe accident evaluation, with some responsibilities deferred to the COL (Combined License) applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0712.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3.A Values of Parameters Used in Analytical Model - Initial Power Level and Modes of Operation
nuscale_ch01_0712.summary: This section confirms that the analytical models used for evaluating events such as loss of external load, turbine trip, loss of condenser vacuum, main steam isolation valve closure, and steam pressure regulator failure (closed) conform to the regulatory criteria outlined in DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5. It specifies that parameters such as initial power level, modes of operation, scram characteristics, core burnup, and instrumentation setpoints are all addressed and conform to requirements, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system elements, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections within the overall reactor system are briefly outlined.

nuscale_ch01_0648.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.5 Plant Characteristics Considered in the Safety Evaluation
nuscale_ch01_0648.summary: Section I.5, "Plant Characteristics Considered in the Safety Evaluation," confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report fully conforms to the relevant regulatory criteria for this aspect of transient and accident analyses. No exceptions or deviations are noted for this section.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC regulations and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and applied, ensuring they meet the conditions specified in relevant NRC regulations and regulatory guides. A consolidated list of the specific ASME code cases used is provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch01_0650.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.7 Evaluation of Individual Initiating Events
nuscale_ch01_0650.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, specifically regarding the evaluation of individual initiating events. It states that the evaluation of individual initiating events fully conforms to the applicable regulatory guidance, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0711.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3 Analytical Model
nuscale_ch01_0711.summary: This section confirms that the analytical models and parameter values (such as initial power level, scram characteristics, core burnup, and instrumentation setpoints) used for evaluating events like loss of external load, turbine trip, and related transients conform to the requirements of DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5. No deviations or exceptions are noted, indicating full compliance with regulatory criteria and standards. The referenced table clarifies that certain SRP/DSRS sections are not applicable to the NuScale design but may apply to plant-specific licensing changes.

edges.description: Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures that visually clarify the design and configuration of systems including the DHRS, while Section B specifies the inspection and testing requirements for DHRS components, connecting them by showing how the design (A) must accommodate and comply with the regulatory and operational requirements outlined in B.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures that visually clarify the design and operation of safety-related systems, while Section B describes the material and environmental conditions (such as vacuum maintenance) that support the reliability and function of those systems, connecting the physical design to its operational environment.

Section A provides detailed drawings and diagrams, including instrumentation and controls, that visually clarify the design and operation of systems such as the DHRS described in Section B, which outlines the design basis and safety function of the Decay Heat Removal System.

Section A provides detailed drawings and diagrams of NuScale's instrumentation and control systems, which are referenced in Section B to demonstrate conformance or partial conformance with specific regulatory criteria for radiation protection design features.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and component information that visually clarify the NuScale plant's design, which supports the comparative analysis in Section B by illustrating how NuScale's features differ from conventional PWRs as listed in the comparison table.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures that visually clarify the design and operation of NuScale's systems, which support the safety considerations and benefits described in Section B regarding the containment vessel (CNV) vacuum and its impact on system reliability and safety.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B describes how maintaining a vacuum in the CNV reduces corrosion and leakage risks for components, thus both sections address measures that enhance the reliability and integrity of safety-related systems and components.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B provides drawings and visual clarifications that can be used to identify and understand the components referenced in Section A.
Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, providing detailed implementation information for the general DHRS design basis described in Section B.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the passive safety systems replacing conventional systems, while Section B outlines the degree of conformance of the NuScale design with radiation protection regulatory criteria, connecting them through the regulatory evaluation of whether the passive systems (including DHRS) meet safety and radiation protection standards.

Section A details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the passive safety systems replacing conventional systems as compared in Section B's table of NuScale versus typical PWR features.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the passive safety systems replacing conventional systems discussed in Section B, where the safety benefits of the CNV vacuum (such as corrosion reduction and leakage detection) support the reliability of those same passive systems.
Section B provides visual and schematic details (such as I&C and electrical drawings) that clarify and support the general design descriptions and material choices discussed in Section A.

Section A describes how maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) reduces moisture and corrosion, which supports the reliability of passive systems like the DHRS described in Section B, thereby supporting the adequacy of passive safety alternatives.

Section A describes the engineered safety feature of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV to minimize corrosion and facilitate leak detection, while Section B lists the NuScale FSAR's conformance (or partial conformance) with regulatory guidance on radiation protection design features, indicating how the design features in A relate to meeting regulatory requirements in B.

Section A describes specific engineered safety feature materials and their benefits in the NuScale design, while Section B provides a comparative overview of NuScale's major design features (including safety systems) against conventional PWRs, thus Section A provides details that exemplify or specify the general design differences highlighted in Section B.

Section A provides electrical and instrumentation drawings that visually clarify the design and operation of systems like the DHRS described in Section B, which details the functional and safety performance requirements for decay heat removal.

Section A provides detailed electrical and I&C drawings that visually clarify the design implementation, which supports Section B's evaluation of conformance with regulatory criteria for radiation protection design features by supplying necessary technical evidence.

Section A provides detailed electrical and I&C drawings that visually clarify the NuScale design features whose nominal parameters and differences from conventional PWRs are summarized in Section B for comparative analysis.

Section A provides detailed drawings and figures of the NuScale plant's electrical and I&C systems, which visually clarify the design features that underpin the safety considerations described in Section B, such as maintaining a vacuum in the CNV to enhance reliability and reduce corrosion.

Section A describes the design basis and safety function of the DHRS, which is a passive safety system replacing conventional systems as referenced in Table 1.3-2, while Section B lists the NuScale FSAR's conformance with regulatory criteria (including partial conformance to radiation protection guidance), thus connecting the DHRS's design and performance to its regulatory adequacy.

Section A describes the design basis and safety function of the NuScale DHRS as a passive alternative to conventional safety systems, while Section B provides a comparative context by listing NuScale's design features (including passive systems) against those of typical PWRs, thus connecting the adequacy of the passive approach to industry norms.

Section A describes the DHRS's capability to remove decay heat and ensure safe shutdown without external power, while Section B outlines the safety benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel, which indirectly supports the DHRS's reliability by reducing corrosion and leakage risks.

Section A details NuScale's conformance with radiation protection regulatory criteria, while Section B compares NuScale's design features and parameters (including safety systems) with those of conventional PWRs, thus connecting through the evaluation of how NuScale's design (including passive safety features) aligns with regulatory expectations and industry norms.

Section A lists regulatory guidance and conformance statements related to radiation protection design features, while Section B describes specific design features of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) that contribute to safety, thus Section B provides concrete design implementations that are evaluated for conformance in Section A.

Section A provides a comparative overview of NuScale's design features relative to conventional PWRs, while Section B details a specific safety-related design feature (the CNV vacuum) that supports the overall safety claims made in Section A.

Section A describes the general plant features and functions of the Central Utility Building, while Section B provides detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and specify the design and arrangement of systems mentioned in Section A.

Section B specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the passive safety systems referenced in Section A's general plant description, thus providing detailed implementation for the general features mentioned.

Section A lists topical and technical reports referenced in the NuScale FSAR, while Section B describes the provision of detailed drawings (such as P&IDs) that visually clarify the design information discussed in those referenced reports.

Section A lists referenced topical reports that include quality assurance and design certification materials, while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, both connecting through the regulatory framework and referenced documentation that underpin the adequacy and compliance of passive safety system design and performance.

Section A lists topical reports and technical references, such as quality assurance and design documentation, that provide supporting background and justification for the engineered safety features described in Section B, which details the containment vessel's vacuum maintenance and its safety benefits.

Section A describes the functional design and operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), while Section B provides the supporting piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify and detail the ECCS components and their configuration.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design and operation, while Section B specifies the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS actuation valves, both of which are passive safety systems intended to replace conventional active systems, thus connecting through their roles in ensuring adequate passive safety per the analysis question.

Section A describes the ECCS's passive decay heat removal function, which relies on venting steam into the CNV, while Section B details the CNV's vacuum conditions that facilitate steam condensation and thus support the ECCS's operation.

Section A provides a general description of facility systems like the SCWS, while Section B offers detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and specify the design and operation of those systems described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall facility and auxiliary cooling systems, while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the design and operational assurance of passive safety system components replacing conventional systems.

Section A describes the facility's cooling systems and reactor core layout, while Section B details the containment vessel's vacuum environment and its safety benefits; both sections provide complementary information about plant design features that contribute to overall safety but do not directly reference or specify each other.

Section A references ASME BPVC code requirements for reactor vessel attachments, while Section B provides drawings and diagrams that visually clarify the design and placement of such components, meaning Section B supports the understanding and verification of the compliance described in Section A.

Section A discusses inspection and examination requirements for reactor vessel attachments per ASME BPVC, while Section B outlines inspection requirements for DHRS components, both referencing compliance with ASME BPVC codes for different system parts, thus connecting through shared regulatory standards for component integrity.

Section B explains that maintaining a vacuum in the CNV eliminates the need for RPV insulation, which is referenced in Section A's discussion of nonstructural attachments and insulation supports on the reactor vessel.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the reactor vessel, which are depicted and clarified in Section B's piping and instrumentation diagrams, connecting the vessel's design details to their visual representation.

Section A describes the reactor vessel as a key pressure-retaining component of the reactor coolant system, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components (including steam piping and valves) that interface with the reactor vessel and coolant system, connecting them through their roles in maintaining system integrity and safety.

Section A describes the reactor vessel materials and their role in the reactor coolant system, while Section B details the containment vessel's vacuum environment, which helps protect the reactor vessel (from Section A) by minimizing corrosion and leakage, thus supporting the integrity and reliability of the reactor vessel materials.

Section A describes specific plant features and structures, while Section B lists the topical and technical reports referenced throughout the FSAR, which may include detailed information supporting the descriptions in Section A.

Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System that replaces conventional safety systems referenced in Section A's discussion of plant features, directly connecting to the analysis question about the adequacy of passive alternatives.

Section A lists the Central Utility Building and its housed systems, while Section B describes the Site Cooling Water System and its role in supporting plant auxiliary systems, both providing context for the plant's support infrastructure relevant to evaluating the adequacy of passive safety alternatives.

Section A lists referenced topical reports that provide the technical and quality assurance basis for the NuScale design, while Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) that is part of the safety approach discussed in those reports, connecting the referenced documentation to the specific system implementation.

Section A lists referenced topical reports, such as those detailing quality assurance, which provide supporting documentation and technical basis for the general plant and system descriptions found in Section B.

Section A lists referenced topical reports and quality assurance documentation that provide the technical and regulatory basis for the NuScale design, while Section B details specific ASME code compliance for reactor vessel attachments, with both sections connected through their reliance on referenced standards and reports to demonstrate regulatory compliance.

Section A lists referenced topical reports, including those that may provide quality assurance or design basis information, while Section B describes the physical configuration and materials of the reactor vessel, with the connection being that referenced reports in A could provide supporting documentation or requirements for the design details described in B.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) that replaces conventional safety systems, while Section B provides a general facility and process overview including the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS), with both sections connected by their roles in the plant's overall heat removal and safety strategy.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design for decay heat removal, while Section B details the reactor vessel's structural and inspection requirements, with both sections connected through the reliance on ASME BPVC code compliance to ensure safety and integrity of passive safety features.

Section A describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design and operation, which relies on the reactor vessel and containment vessel materials and configuration detailed in Section B, as the ECCS function depends on the integrity and arrangement of these reactor vessel components.

Section A describes the general plant and system layout, including the reactor core and cooling systems, while Section B details the reactor vessel's compliance with ASME code requirements for attachments and welds, connecting them through the physical and regulatory design features that ensure structural integrity and safety.

Section A provides a general overview of the facility and its systems, including the reactor core's location and support assembly, while Section B specifies the detailed composition and configuration of the reactor vessel that houses the core described in Section A.

Section A provides specific inspection and welding requirements for reactor vessel attachments, which are components described more generally in Section B's overview of the reactor vessel and its materials.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B provides detailed P&ID drawings that visually clarify the design and instrumentation relevant to those operator actions, thus supporting the evaluation of whether the design meets the regulatory criteria.

Section B provides specific design and inspection details for DHRS components, which are relevant to Section A's regulatory review criteria for evaluating whether passive safety systems (like DHRS) and associated operator actions meet defense-in-depth requirements.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B describes design features of the NuScale containment vessel that passively enhance safety by reducing corrosion and debris, thus providing context for evaluating whether passive alternatives (as referenced in the analysis question) can satisfy regulatory expectations for safety.

Section A establishes review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in safety analyses, while Section B provides detailed P&ID drawings that visually clarify the design and instrumentation relevant to those operator actions, thus Section B supports the application of Section A's criteria by supplying necessary design context.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the regulatory assurance that both human and equipment reliability are addressed in the NuScale safety approach.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B describes the design features of the NuScale containment vessel that passively enhance safety by minimizing corrosion and heat transfer, thus supporting the adequacy of passive safety systems in lieu of conventional ones.

Section A references regulatory criteria and design limits for thermal margins and fuel safety, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify the design and instrumentation supporting those criteria.

Section A discusses how NuScale ensures thermal margin and fuel safety by adhering to regulatory criteria, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components that are part of the passive safety systems replacing conventional ones, thus both sections are connected through the assurance of safety margins and regulatory compliance for the passive systems' performance.

Section A discusses how NuScale's design meets regulatory thermal margin limits for control rod misoperation scenarios, while Section B describes the engineered safety feature of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) to enhance reliability and reduce corrosion, both of which contribute to the overall safety case for replacing conventional safety systems with passive alternatives.

Section A outlines regulatory requirements and conformance for safety systems such as DHRS, while Section B provides the detailed Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams that visually clarify how these systems are implemented in the NuScale design.

Section A states that the DHRS must be safety grade and automatically initiated, while Section B provides evidence that the DHRS components meet preservice and inservice inspection requirements and are classified appropriately, supporting the claim that the DHRS fulfills regulatory safety requirements.

Section A specifies regulatory requirements for safety-grade and automatically initiated DHRS systems, while Section B describes design features of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) that support the reliability and performance of passive safety systems like DHRS by minimizing corrosion and facilitating leak detection.

Section A (SRP 19.0 and related regulatory criteria) establishes the probabilistic risk assessment and severe accident evaluation requirements that Section B's Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams must support by providing detailed design information for review.

Section B provides specific design and inspection details for DHRS components, which are relevant to Section A's broader regulatory criteria for safety and risk assessment, thereby supporting the evaluation of whether the passive DHRS alternatives meet the safety requirements discussed in Section A.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and acceptance standards for risk assessment and defense-in-depth, while Section B describes specific design features (vacuum in the CNV) that contribute to safety and reliability, thus providing evidence that supports the regulatory compliance discussed in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and acceptance standards for evaluating the technical adequacy of risk assessments and human factors in plant modifications, while Section B provides the detailed design drawings and diagrams that serve as the basis for such evaluations, connecting the regulatory requirements to the actual system configurations.

Section B provides specific design and inspection details for DHRS components, which are relevant to evaluating whether the NuScale passive safety approach (referenced in Section A's regulatory conformance context) meets the technical adequacy and safety level required by regulatory criteria.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and acceptance standards for evaluating risk-informed changes and defense-in-depth, while Section B describes specific design features (vacuum in the CNV) that support the reliability and safety of passive engineered safety features, thus providing evidence relevant to meeting the regulatory expectations in Section A.

Section A references and builds upon the review criteria established in Section B, indicating that the Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation) criteria are applied after the Phase 1 (Analysis) criteria from the same regulatory appendix.

Section A discusses the review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B outlines the thermal margin and fuel safety limits that must be met during control rod misoperation events; both sections are connected through the regulatory requirement that passive or manual safety measures (as in NuScale's approach) must demonstrably maintain fuel integrity and thermal margins under accident scenarios.

Section A discusses the regulatory review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B specifies that the DHRS must be safety grade and automatically initiated, connecting them through the regulatory requirement that passive safety systems (like DHRS) must not rely on manual operator actions to meet safety criteria.

Section A and Section B both address the applicability of human factors engineering review criteria and the crediting of manual operator actions in the context of NuScale's safety analysis, with both sections concluding that certain acceptance criteria are not applicable to new reactor designs like NuScale's, thus aligning their regulatory interpretations.

Section A and Section B both reference the applicability of SRP Appendix 18-A and SRP 18.0 criteria to NuScale's safety analysis, specifically noting that the human factors engineering review criteria for modifications affecting risk-important human actions are not applicable to NuScale's current licensing basis.

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B outlines the thermal margin and fuel safety limits that must be maintained during control rod misoperation events; both sections are connected through their focus on ensuring that human actions and system performance collectively satisfy safety requirements for accident scenarios.

Section A discusses the regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, while Section B specifies that the DHRS must be safety grade and automatically initiated, connecting them through the requirement that passive safety systems (like DHRS) reduce reliance on manual actions to meet regulatory safety expectations.

Section A and Section B both address the applicability of human factors engineering review criteria to NuScale's design, specifically noting that certain acceptance criteria for manual operator actions are not applicable to new reactors like NuScale, thereby supporting the regulatory evaluation of passive safety system adequacy.

Section A and Section B both address the applicability of human factors engineering review criteria to NuScale's design, specifically noting that certain acceptance criteria for human actions are not applicable to new designs like NuScale, thus establishing a shared regulatory context for evaluating the adequacy of passive safety systems.

Section A describes how NuScale's design meets thermal margin and fuel integrity limits for control rod misoperation events, while Section B outlines regulatory criteria for thermal margin limits and DHRS requirements during similar transients, connecting them through the shared focus on safety analysis and conformance to regulatory thermal margin and system performance requirements.

Section A details how NuScale's design meets specific thermal margin and fuel safety criteria by using passive safety features, while Section B addresses the broader probabilistic risk assessment and human factors considerations for new reactors, both sections connecting through their evaluation of whether passive systems and associated human actions provide adequate safety in the absence of conventional systems.

Section A discusses NuScale's conformance with thermal margin and fuel safety criteria for control rod events, while Section B addresses the technical adequacy of risk assessments and human factors for risk-informed licensing, both connecting through their evaluation of whether NuScale's passive safety approach meets regulatory safety requirements.

Section A specifies regulatory requirements for the safety classification and automatic initiation of the DHRS, while Section B provides the probabilistic risk assessment framework and human factors considerations that evaluate whether the NuScale passive safety approach (including DHRS performance) meets overall safety objectives.

Section A specifies that the DHRS must be safety grade and automatically initiated, while Section B references the technical adequacy of risk assessments and human factors for plant modifications, connecting them through the requirement that passive safety systems (like DHRS) must meet both deterministic safety criteria and risk-informed regulatory expectations.

Section A (SRP 19.0) establishes the general probabilistic risk assessment and severe accident evaluation criteria for new reactors, while Section B (SRP 19.1) specifies the technical adequacy requirements for such assessments in the context of risk-informed license amendments, thus Section B provides more detailed requirements that support and elaborate on the general framework set by Section A.

Section A specifies the analytical model parameters and operational modes used to evaluate safety system performance, while Section B provides the detailed P&ID drawings that visually clarify the design and configuration of those systems, thus Section B provides the technical context for the parameters and systems analyzed in Section A.

Section A discusses the analytical model parameters and regulatory conformance for transient events, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the reliance on DHRS performance as a passive safety system in the analytical model's evaluation of safety adequacy.

Section A specifies the analytical model parameters and regulatory conformance for transient events, while Section B describes the engineered safety feature (CNV vacuum) that supports the passive safety approach analyzed in Section A.

Section A describes the operational sequence and component functions for DHRS operation, while Section B provides the corresponding drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and support the textual descriptions in Section A.

Section A describes operational steps and system preparation involving the DHRS, while Section B specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, connecting them through the operational reliance on DHRS performance and the regulatory assurance of its integrity.

Section A describes operational steps involving the containment vessel (CNV) and pressure adjustments during safety system transitions, while Section B explains the rationale and benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the CNV, which underpins the operational procedures in Section A.

Section A outlines the plant characteristics and regulatory conformance considered in safety evaluations, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams (including I&C) that visually clarify and support the design information referenced in Section A.

Section A discusses the general safety evaluation criteria and regulatory conformance for NuScale's plant characteristics, while Section B provides specific details on the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, thereby specifying how the general safety evaluation in A is implemented for the DHRS described in B.

Section B describes the design features (e.g., vacuum in the CNV) that support the reliability and performance of passive safety systems, which are evaluated in Section A for their adequacy in meeting regulatory safety criteria.

Section A references compliance with ASME and NRC codes for overpressure protection, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify the design and implementation of such systems, meaning Section B supports and specifies the technical details referenced in Section A.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME BPVC and NRC requirements for overpressure protection, while Section B specifies that DHRS components, including actuation valves, are subject to preservice and inservice inspections per Section XI of the BPVC, thus both sections are connected through their reliance on ASME BPVC codes for ensuring the integrity and inspection of safety-related components.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME and NRC codes for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B describes how maintaining a vacuum in the CNV supports material reliability and leak detection, both contributing to the overall safety and integrity of the reactor coolant boundary.

Section A outlines the regulatory evaluation criteria and classification for transient and accident analyses, which are applied to the design details and system configurations depicted in Section B's piping and instrumentation diagrams to assess compliance and adequacy of safety systems.

Section B provides specific design and inspection details for DHRS components, which are relevant to Section A's evaluation of whether NuScale's passive safety systems (including DHRS) conform to regulatory safety analysis requirements.

Section B describes specific design features of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) that support the reliability and effectiveness of passive safety systems, which are evaluated in Section A for their adequacy in replacing conventional safety systems.

Section A references analytical models and parameter values used to demonstrate regulatory conformance for specific transient events, while Section B provides the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify the system configurations and components analyzed in Section A.

Section A discusses the analytical modeling and regulatory conformance of NuScale's safety system performance during specific transients, while Section B details the inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components that are part of the passive safety alternatives evaluated in Section A, thus connecting the analytical assessment of system adequacy with the operational reliability of its components.

Section A discusses analytical models and parameter values for specific transient events, while Section B describes the design features and benefits of maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), with the analytical models in A relying on the containment conditions described in B as part of their assumptions and boundary conditions.

Section A specifies the analytical model parameters and regulatory conformance for transient events, while Section B describes the operational sequence and system components (including DHRS) that are analyzed using those parameters, connecting the model's assumptions to the actual system behavior.

Section A provides specific analytical model parameters and conformance details for event analyses that are categorized and introduced in Section B's broader discussion of transient and accident analysis methodology.

Section A provides the initial conditions and analytical model parameters for transient events, which are necessary inputs for evaluating the overpressure protection requirements and code compliance described in Section B.

Section A provides specific analytical model parameters and conformance details for particular transient events, while Section B outlines the general approach and criteria for categorizing and evaluating such events, thus Section A specifies details for the broader framework described in Section B.

Section A provides specific values of parameters (such as initial power level and modes of operation) that are used within the broader analytical model described in Section B, thereby detailing the inputs for the model's application to safety analysis.

Section A describes specific operational steps and system configurations for the NuScale DHRS, while Section B outlines the regulatory framework and criteria (DSRS 15.0) used to evaluate the adequacy of such safety systems, connecting them through the requirement that the described system performance must satisfy the regulatory safety evaluation criteria.

Section A describes operational steps and system configurations for the DHRS and reactor coolant system, while Section B outlines compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory codes for reactor coolant boundary integrity, connecting them through the requirement that the system components and operations in A must satisfy the regulatory and code compliance detailed in B.

Section A describes specific operational steps and system configurations for the NuScale DHRS, while Section B outlines the regulatory criteria and classification approach for transient and accident analyses that these system operations must satisfy.

Section A describes specific operational steps and system configurations for the NuScale DHRS during transients, while Section B outlines the regulatory analytical model and parameter values required to evaluate system performance under those same transient scenarios, connecting them through the need to demonstrate that the described system operations (A) conform to the regulatory analysis framework (B).

Section A outlines the plant characteristics and safety evaluation criteria, which set the context for assessing whether the overpressure protection measures described in Section B (including compliance with ASME and NRC requirements) are sufficient to meet regulatory safety expectations.

Section A outlines the plant characteristics and general conformance with regulatory criteria considered in the safety evaluation, while Section B details the evaluation of individual initiating events using the same regulatory framework, thus Section A provides the context and prerequisites for the specific analyses performed in Section B.

Section A provides the general plant characteristics and event categorization framework that underpins the specific analytical models and parameter values detailed in Section B for transient and accident analyses.

Section A describes compliance with ASME and NRC codes for overpressure protection, which is a prerequisite for the accident and transient analyses framework outlined in Section B, as proper system integrity must be ensured before credible accident analysis can be performed.

Section A describes compliance with ASME and NRC code requirements for overpressure protection, which is a prerequisite for the accident analyses and parameter values used in Section B's analytical models for transient events.
